title: German Occupation Policy in Europe, 1939–1945
subtitle: Administration, Exploitation, and Terror
author: VeteransLedger Editorial
summary: Nazi Germany's occupation of Europe was not a uniform phenomenon but a hierarchy of racial ideology, economic exploitation, and terror that ranged from satellite alliances to systematic genocide.

body.content: By mid-1942, Germany exercised direct or indirect control over most of continental Europe from the English Channel to the Volga, from Norway's North Cape to the Libyan desert. The administration of this empire was never rationalised into a coherent system — instead it reflected competing Nazi ideological priorities, the particular racial status assigned to each conquered people, and the competing ambitions of occupation administrators.

Nazi ideology dictated that different peoples merited different treatment. At the favoured extreme stood the 'Germanic' peoples — Danes, Norwegians, Dutch, and Flemish — who were envisioned as potential members of a Greater German Reich and treated with relative restraint, though subject to increasingly harsh reprisals as resistance mounted. Denmark retained its government and monarchy until August 1943.

Western European peoples — French, Belgians, and others — were exploited economically but generally administered through existing institutions when possible, as in Vichy France. This 'collaboration' model minimised occupation costs while extracting labour, food, industrial output, and eventually workers for German factories.

At the opposite extreme stood the peoples of Eastern Europe and the Soviet Union. The Generalplan Ost — a secret SS planning document drafted in 1941 — envisioned the deportation or murder of 30–45 million Slavs over 25 years, the Germanisation of the remainder, and the settlement of ethnic Germans across an 'Eastern living space'. This ideological framework shaped occupation policies that produced mass starvation, slave labour, and genocide.

Across occupied Europe, Germany extracted enormous economic resources. France alone paid occupation 'costs' of 400 million Reichsmarks per day — far exceeding actual German administrative expenses — effectively subsidising the German war economy. Occupied Soviet territories were stripped of grain, livestock, and industrial equipment. The Reichskommissariat Ukraine, administered by Erich Koch, was run on the explicit principle that Ukrainians should receive only enough food to maintain their capacity to work for Germany.

The forced labour programme mobilised over 8 million foreign workers for German industry by 1944. Fritz Sauckel, appointed Plenipotentiary General for Labour Deployment in 1942, organised the deportation of workers from across occupied Europe — initially with offers of wages and rations, later by outright force. Workers from Western Europe received marginally better conditions; Soviet and Polish workers were classified as subhuman and subjected to systematic brutality. The death rate among Soviet forced labourers in Germany approached that of concentration camp inmates.

Across occupied Europe, resistance was met with systematic collective reprisals. The Wehrmacht and SS operated under orders — formalised in Hitler's Commando Order (October 1942) and the Nacht und Nebel ('Night and Fog') decree (December 1941) — that mandated execution of resistance fighters and the disappearance of suspects into the concentration camp system. The ratio of hostages executed per German killed varied: in Serbia, the Wehrmacht applied a ratio of 100 Serbs per German soldier killed.

In France, the June 1944 massacre at Oradour-sur-Glane (642 civilians killed by the 2nd SS Panzer Division Das Reich) and the SS reprisals following the assassination of Reinhard Heydrich in Czechoslovakia (the destruction of Lidice and Ležáky) exemplify the systematic use of terror against civilian populations. In the East, the scale was incomparably greater — entire villages were regularly burned with their inhabitants.
The Holocaust within the Occupation Framework

The occupation apparatus was inseparable from the Final Solution. The Wannsee Conference (January 1942) coordinated the deportation of Jews from across occupied Europe to the extermination centres in the East — Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibór, Bełżec, Chełmno, and Majdanek. The participation of local police forces, collaborationist administrations (particularly in France, the Netherlands, and the Baltic states), and railway authorities across occupied Europe was essential to the logistics of the genocide.

Of the approximately six million Jews murdered in the Holocaust, the overwhelming majority lived in territories under German occupation or control. The intersection of racial ideology, administrative structures, and war-fuelled radicalism produced a crime of unprecedented systematic scale.

Occupied populations responded across a spectrum. Active collaboration — from economic cooperation to participation in persecution — was widespread, motivated by ideology, opportunity, or coercion. Active resistance was practised by minorities who organised intelligence networks, partisan units, and escape lines at enormous personal risk. The vast majority navigated between, attempting to survive.

The postwar reckoning with collaboration was uneven. France mythologised its resistance while concealing the extent of Vichy participation in Jewish deportations. Norway prosecuted Quislingites while rehabilitating economic collaborators. The Netherlands confronted collaboration in deportations only decades after the war. The complexities of occupation — the coercion involved, the range of motivations, and the impossibility of judging moral choices made under life-threatening pressure — continue to generate historical debate.

archival_note: Bundesarchiv, Koblenz. R 70 series: Reich Security Main Office (RSHA) occupation administration records. Bundesarchiv Berlin-Lichterfelde NS 19: Himmler personal staff — Generalplan Ost. Centre for Holocaust and Genocide Studies, Amsterdam: Records of the Reichskommissariat Niederlande.

sources.ref: Bundesarchiv Berlin-Lichterfelde. NS 19/1739: Generalplan Ost — draft and comments, 1941–1942.
National Archives (UK). FO 371: Foreign Office records — occupied territories reports, 1940–1945.
Mazower, Mark. Hitler's Empire: Nazi Rule in Occupied Europe. Allen Lane, 2008.
sources.note: The definitive survey of Nazi occupation policy across Europe.

sources.ref: Herbert, Ulrich. Hitler's Foreign Workers: Enforced Foreign Labor in Germany under the Third Reich. Cambridge University Press, 1997.
Browning, Christopher. Ordinary Men: Reserve Police Battalion 101 and the Final Solution in Poland. HarperCollins, 1992.
